text: At night in the damp darkness,
It was night in the damp darkness,
Damp darkness when winds cease their howl.
Spoke he royally, the Creator of broad courtyards.
Come see him speak, the Creator of good fortune .
I heard him say, "Lushuli,
Lushuli, Uprooter of underbrush,
Quickly! Find for me Banoina,
Banoina, the Royal chef.
Have him come and counsel me.
This day I would be counselled."
He went, he called him, Lushuli,
He called him among the Balwani.
"He calls you, the Creator of broad courtyards,
He calls you, the Creator of good fortune."
He answered, "Your will be done, O Sun ."
That day he came running, truly, truly.
[Enanga Solo]
He asked no other word Bainiona.
What has taken hold of you, my king?"
He said, "What has taken hold of me, my man–
Now see, I am confounded, truly, truly–
What has become of the Muhyoza,
Raphia skirt Nyamugenya?
For eight days now
They have not come to greet the Hunter of the steppes.
I fear the Muhyoza,
I fear, my man."
He answered, "Hold and let me counsel you,
My king."
He said, "When we judge it to be daybreak,
Daybreak–the giant sun is a gout of blood–
We'll sound, we'll salute the Roan bull.
We'll salute the Obliterator of borders.
We'll raise a roar with the Roan bull.
Then we'll see how they come out for war, the Muhyoza,
Raphia skirt Nyamugenya."
When they judged it to be daybreak,
Daybreak–the giant sun is a gout of blood–
They sounded the Obliterator of borders.
That day they raised a roar with the Roan bull.
They listened, the Muhyoza,
Raphia skirt Nyamugenya.
"What has happened to the King of Kabale,
King Hunter of the steppes?
Since they did not beat the Leper . . ."
[Interruption–children playing too loudly shooed
"What has happened, Father-creator ,
My king?"
He said, "Quickly! To the courtyard at the rocky outcrop!
It is I who have all facts."
They attended, the Mulalamba troop,
Who drive all before them .
"What has happened? What has happened,
O Bull who stamps smudge fires? "
He said, "Quickly! To the rocky outcrop!
It is I who will make known all facts."
They went and gathered on the rocky outcrop,
Raphia skirt Nyamugenya.
They gathered, the Nourished-by-the-Clash-of-Battle troop,
Big foot of Lwanda.
They gathered, the Mirth-makers troop.
"It is I," he said, "who will make known the facts."
They gathered on the rocky outcrop.
At Kabahula, that very place.
He arose, the Creator of broad courtyards,
The Creator of good fortune.
I saw him in the center of the palace forecourt.
At the fire of the Brown-flecked Bull,
At the iron bars that chime a warning,
He asked no questions coming.
Into the palace courtyard,
With folded arms he came.
His eyes sought not the Bahyoza.
His eyes sought Kagondo village.
"Long life, Creator of broad courtyards !"
Then they fell in a stony silence.
"Long life, Unwavering one !"
Then they fell in a stony silence.
They saluted, the Nourished by the Clash of Battle,
And they also fell in a stony silence.
He said, "What has become of you, Bahyoza,
That you speak no words to me?"
They said, "We hunger, O Sun.
We hunger, we are pained to hunger so.
We lack the land for growing sorgham:
Lord, that is why we hunger so"
He said, "And I had been confounded, Muhyoza,
I had been confounded, my men.
You'd gone for eight full days
Without once greeting the Hunter of the steppes ."
He summoned Ngimbwa, Ngimbwa,
"Ngimbwa, my man,
Go, be quick, be quick,
Be quick and go for me to the kingdom of Kiziba.
Go and tell him, the Owner of bottom land,
Tell him, Calfs like iron hammers,
Tell him, Hafted knife that cleaves great trees,
Axe that makes the forest roar,
Child of Born among kings and Breast-giving comforter
Wilderness, he comes from Kishuga Ngalama.
Say, 'By that blood brotherhood
Sworn by Kitekele and Luyobya:'
Say, 'They hunger, the Bahyoza,
They hunger, the Raphia skirt Nyamugenya.
They lack the land for growing sorgham:
They are pained to hunger so.'"
He asked no further word, Ngimbwa.
He summoned his child, Kajoki.
"Kajoki, Kajoki
Kajoki, my child,
Go, be quick, travel home to Katoma.
Speak to your mother, Kaiyula.
Say, "He'll be brought this way with Muhyoza
Tomorrow. The king has sent him to Kiziba.'"
The child asked no other word.
He travelled, he went and was quick.
He travelled to his father's house.
He found his mother, Kaiyula.
He said, "Father is behind me. He'll come with Muhyoza.
The king has sent him to Kiziba."
At the time she was there,
Kaiyula, Kaiyula, Kaiyula . . .
She cooked some yams,
Yams she had harvested in Itahwa.
She peeled the yams.
She hurried and put them in a basket.
Calabashes of beer that day,
She set out truly, truly.
At the time she was there, there, there,
She was surprised to see Bahyoza.
Among them was Ngimbwa, Ngimbwa, Ngimbwa,
Bad dream of the Makuba.
An ancient feud fixed a scar above his eye.
Child of Bushalita,
And Settler of disputes.
They call him Child of Muyego maiden, Long-necked giraffe.
He said, "My wife." She said, "My lord."
He said, "I've come my wife."
She said, "I give you greeting, my lord."
She gives greetings to the men of the Hunter of the steppes.
A harvest basket
Of yams she brought,
With serving basket to the dining place.
With banana-pith towel they towel their hands.
They eat the yams.
A pipe-full of tobacco they wish to puff.
A calabash of beer . . .
That day they had no need to ask.
They say, "We're going now,
We're going now, my wife."
They descend to Kijululuzi village.
Past Kempongwa hill they march their march.
At Kigalama of Entahi,
They did not ask a word.
At that very place, that very place,
They did not speak a word.
At Byeshekaima at the home of Kishenya,
At Kyambu, Kyambu, Kyambu,
I saw them truly, truly cross the water.
At Kinoba village,
I saw them climb their climb.
At Kashaka of the copses,
I saw them push on to Katenda.
At Kashaka of the copses,
That day at Kyendanzigu as well,
At Katendagulo, just there,
I saw their determined march.
Through the Swamp of Defeat,
They marched their march.
At the Steppe of the Wild Figs,
I saw their determined march.
In Kijunja vilage, that very place,
I saw their determined march.
They did not ask another word.
The king,
Owner of bottom land, Great-calfed one
In Kijunja, that very place,
Is where the palace then was.
They did not ask a thing.
At the courtyard gate they met them.
The officer of the watch was Balongo.
"Long life, Balongo."
That day he returned their respect truly, truly.
"Where do you come from, Bahyoza?"
They said, "We come from the Hunter of the steppes.
He says he salutes, he salutes,
His desire is to come and salute."
They did not ask another word.
They went and told the king.
He said, "Quickly! Let them come in, the men of the Great-calfed one,
Of the Ancient boar, child of Makes hunters wary. "
As they still approached, "Respectful greetings,
Live forever, O Sun.
He salutes you, he salutes you,
The Bull who stamps smudge fires.
He would like you to give him land for growing sorgham.
The Bahyoza are pained to hunger so.
He would like you to remember the blood brotherhood
Sworn by Kitekele and Luyobya."
[Enanga solo]
He did not ask another word.
He said, "Go cook plantains for them."
They cooked plantains. They ate.
They wished to bid farewell.
He said, "Let them go and cultivate Kashaka of the copses.
They will turn it to bare earth.
I tell you, all of Kitendagulo
Will they turn to bare earth.
All of Lukulungo is theirs.
Let them turn it to bare earth.
And Gera,
Let them turn to bare earth.
I tell you, Kashalazi,
Let them totally redden it red."
They thank him, the Bahyoza,
They then take the road.
They reach the king of Kabale,
They wish to speak.
"He said, My Lord, go cultivate,
Cultivate Kiziba.
All of Kashaka,
He says the King should turn to bare earth.
All of Lukulungo,
Also will he turn to bare earth.
All of Gera,
Also will redden completely red."
He sounded the drum. They attended. "Quickly! Go and dig!" he said.
They dug, the men of Kaitaba.
After they dug, they broadcast seed.
They chop weeds briskly, briskly.
The sorgham ripens.
They yearn to steal in great harvest baskets.
But others come, they tattle.
They say, "Lord, Hunter of the steppes,
While you sit here,
Bahyoza finish off your sorgham."
He said, "I am fearful. Go and find me
Katogoita, child of Kibingaila of Igombe village.
Find me Katogoita,
Katogoita, child of Kibingaila of Igombe village.
Have him come draw out the sorcery. The Baziba do not like us.
They would kill my men."
They went and they summoned him,
Katogoita, child of Kibingaila of Igombe village.
He came, he drew out the sorcery that day.
He did not leave a bit.
They reap the sorgham, they reap it,
They reap it. But of the gleanings,
They do not set aside a royal portion.
Of the wrapped-up bundles
They come and set aside the royal portion,
Which, when they stored it in the rear pantry,
Reached to the royal vestibule.
When they drank it, it seized them:
Bahyoza want the words and deeds of war.
If he with kingly gestures drew attention to himself, they'd say, "You are the Cutter of cattle herds .
If you drew our attention to a bamboo grove, we'd cut it truly, truly."
If with kingly gestures he drew attention to himself, they'd say, "You are the Cutter of cattle herds .
If you drew our attention to a forest, we'd cut it swiftly, swiftly."
He said, "Tell me of our neighbors,
Tell me of Bugengele villagers,
At the home of Nyawela,
At the home of the king's man Byalushengo.
Others spoke in the courtyard guard-house
Called the place of the Balwani clan.
With the force that brought them there they said, "The king has drunk a lot of beer!
He might free us to mount a raid.
For many days now, Bahyoza
We have not eaten a bit of meat,
And this eating without sauce is killing us.
Go make him think that he's been slandered mightily, mightily, mightily."
[Enanga Solo]
They did not ask another word.
I saw them come mightily, mightily, mightily.
With the force that brought them, "Live forever, Father-creator ,
Live forever, Bull who stamps smudge fires.
Live forever, Provider ,
Live forever, you are Owner of the drum and of salutations of respect.
Live forever, Ancient boar,
Live forever, child of Makes hunters wary.
Live forever, Leaf of the Mushula plant,
Live forever, Bull who stamps smudge fires.
He answered, "Tell me of the news abroad,
Of Bugengele, Byalushengo's home,
Of Musila village of Luhungu,
Of Ntoboola and Bugengele."
They said, "Lord, as we sit here eating and drinking,
The Muhamba studies slander."
They say, "He has abused you, Nkwelele,
Lord, Defeater of attempts to turn him back
And more are you abused by Bitalaho of Mujwiga,
And King Kahigi himself abuses you,
The Orphan of Lugomola.
He says the dominion of Kabale
He'll dominate from Mayondwe.
He says he is the Lake that swallows rivers
Especialy those of He who speaks with a bullish roar.
He says they could not salute Bwogi,
Then turn and salute you.
They could not salute Ilimamoshe,
Then turn and salute Milembe."
[Enanga Solo]
I saw when the king unblinked his eyes,
That day, his pupils refused to be shut away again.
He said, "He heaps an elephant on me in abuse and then throws in the trap.
Ebibyalwa herb he changes me to a plant soon to bear.
He said, "He abuses me, that Kahigi
Orphans that no longer help one another."
I heard him say, "What shall I do, What shall I do, What shall I do?"
He said, "My wife Milembe, Milembe, Milembe,
You, my wife, Milembe,"
He said, "Child of Kagoloile
Of the Unbreached shields of Nyamatiti."
He said, "Quickly bring my bow and quiver.
On this very day, I'll go plundering at Migongo."
He said, "He has abused me, that Kahigi.
This day I will exact from him the tribute of the full-grown bull."
She said, "Where do you go to raid, O King?
Quickly set my heart at ease."
He said, "I'm raiding your own Kihanja.
This day I will exact from him the tribute of the full grown bull."
She said, "Do not raid Kihanja like a fool,
Or as though it were a game."
She said, "He has his Mujumba troop, Cause of long-term injuries
And he has never used them up and never have they moved away.
She said, "He has men of Busingo and Bulembo villages.
There are as well the Death-Dealing Spearshafts."
She said, "And those he has never used up,
And those never have moved away."
She said, "If you raid foolishly,
The Muhamba knows how to play the game."
[Enanga Solo]
I heard him say, "You would depose me, wife of mine.
You would depose me, my wife Milembe."
She said, "The word that would depose you, be lost forever!
Quickly! Go and set the land aflame. Turn it to bare earth."
I heard him summon. He summoned him,
Trial-maker of Deserted-one.
He said, "Roil up the oracle waters, let me see.
Let empayula roil up the waters."
When they had roiled them up they saw a royal dairy room, a dairy room.
They saw them in the dairy churning,
In Mwejanjala village infusing the churns with smoke.
He summoned Okyela,
Okyela of Kagenzi Buhunga.
He said, "Go sieze the rooster that crows at dawn
Before the royal bathers have brought my water."
Then he summoned Porcupine Ncheeka
Of Balilemwa Buhunga.
He said, "Quickly! You too! Do the oracle of grass.
This very day I want to go to war."
When they consulted it, they saw them in the dairy churning,
In Mweyanjala village infusing smoke.
He said, "Enough! Close up the chickens! I go to war!
All that's left is to descend on Bugingo's village."
In Kabale he did not rest that night.
He marched and rested in Bugengele.
I saw him descend.
The king did not ask at all.
My lord, my lord, he descended that day,
He did not ask at all.
There he is at Nsisha.
There he is at Kigoye.
There he is at Kaishelukindo.
Iyo-o at Byazi.
And Matembe.
Let me tell you: at Kihilila and Kyebyazi,
At Kanyinayo,
Let me tell you: Paddles caught them on the hip.
He did not ask another word.
Iyo-o at Kishogo,
At Kyaisil' engo,
The king spent eight days there:
Lo! he does not wish to go to war.
[Interruption]
The king spent eight days there:
He never spoke a warlike word.
Again they said, "What can we do with him?
Again let's make him think that he's been slandered."
They said, "Although you've come,
The Muhamba has abused you.
Abused you, the sardonic Muhamba has,
He says, my lord, you did not come for war.
When your brother Kabushumba (died),
My lord, you came as an intruder.
He says you came to intrude inside, my lord,
He says, my lord, you did not come for war.
He says you came when beans were being planted,
Now lo! they are soon to bear.
You came when Planted-ones were being planted,
Lo! now they are thick with foliage.
He says the dominion of Kabale–he himself,
He'll dominate from Mayondwe.
He says one kingship is like another–he himself,
He doesn't fear a thing.
He abused you, that Lukwelele,
The Defeater of attempts to turn him back.
You were abused by the child of Galengaine.
You were abused even more by Balongo
And by that medium of Mugasha you made payment to.
They say they could not salute Bwogi,
And then salute you."
Rage took hold of him.
He said, "Send to Bwogi,
Child of Unbreached shields of Nyamatiti."
[Enanga Solo]
They sent to Bwogi
Child of Unbreached shields of Nyamatiti."
They said, "Stand at Kalabagaine and see
How he has gone to set the land aflame."
He said, "And to my wife send also,
Let her not say, 'He set the land aflame in time of peace.'"
When dawn appeared he set flame to Ndama.
He turned and set flame to Kabilizi.
He went to Slaughter Mountain and set it aflame.
He returned and set flame to Slaughter Mountain.
When he heard of this, Kahigi,
Heard he Kahigi, Nomadic beast of the steppes.
Gluey gum tree you fall upon the launderers.
They touched my head. Dead of night he fears not.
He said, "What shall I do, my friend? I hear royal drums
That seem to me like those of The Hunter of the Steppes. "
He said, "Shall I pay tribute in cattle?
Or shall I pay in weaponry?"
They say, "Lord, pay the tribute in cattle.
Weaponry, Lord, is never paid in tribute."
To his man, Goatskin
He said, "Quickly. Go and salute him.
Say, 'When you were raiding Ihangilo,
Or Buzinza of Mankolongo,
You would send to him, and he would cook Bahyoza plantains,
So they could end their hunger.'
Say, 'Why now did you not send to him, so he could cook Bahyoza
Plantains to end their hunger?'"
Goatskin did not ask another word.
He came fearfully.
And when he cast his eyes on him,
(Calfs) that do not hinder, Thighs that bring no shame
"Long life to the Calfs
Of He who stamps smudge fires.
Long life to Those that do not hinder.
Long life to the Wide strider of the steppe.
Kahigi salutes you.
The Orphan of Lugamola salutes you.
He says when you were raiding Ihangilo
Or Buzinja of Mankolongo,
You would send to him,
So he could cook Bahyoza plantains.
Why now did you not send to him
So he could cook Bahyoza plantains?"
He answered, "Say to him, 'A messinger is never slain,
Otherwise, this day, today, he would have had me killed.'
Say, 'He is not raiding Ihangilo
Or Buzinja of Mankolongo.'
Say, 'You abused him. Now this day
He will make you pay the tribute of the full-grown bull.
He says that when you drank your milk you spit up on your belly.
You spoke again and you abused him.
He says you abused him elephantinely; you badly threw him down.
You transformed him, transformed him to a plant soon to bear.
He says now, this day, today,
He's not going to Buzinja.'"
The man then went. He told this to Kahigi,
The Orphan of Lugomola.
Kahigi took a hundred and a hundred and a hundred cattle. "These are Bwogi's," he said.
"And these cattle are Matiti's."
He took a hundred and a hundred. "These are to swallow your anger,
My king" he said.
He took a hundred and a hundred. "Cut a long staff from the forest," he said,
"A short one puts an end to men."
He said, "These cattle are Bwogi's
And these cattle are Milembe's."
He said, "Let him go slaughter them now, that one.
That one will have nothing more to say."
There they were driven by Kikwelele,
The king's man, Defeater of attempts to turn him back.
There they were driven by Kishumi, Kishumi,
Who wipes the dust from a bull's shoulders.
With what force they greeted as they still approached!
"Live forever Bull that stamps smudge fires ."
He said, "You? Who are you?"
He said, "I am Kishumi, I wipe the dust from a bull's shoulders. "
He said, "Now why did you abuse me?
This day I just might kill you, my man."
He continued, "Sir, if you wipe the dust from a bull's shoulders,
Come and wipe my face."
He answered, "What would there be to wipe, Father-creator ?
Surely a Hinda woman wiped it long ago."
He said, "But why did you abuse me?"
He answered, "My lord, may the word be lost forever
That slanders a king like you."
And Lukwelele said the same.
That day they denied blame swiftly, swiftly.
He said, "Go and tell Kahigi.
Say, 'He has forgiven you this day.
When you hear the small drum sound from Kanguge,
When he returns, he will make you pay the tribute of the full-grown bull.
When he is returning from the raid,
Do not take aim on him from a mountain overlook.'"
[An audience member recites his own self praise]
I am the weakened one who stirs things about.
Thunder said, "I'll strike you!" I said,"You strike me?
I am the grower of sharpened points.
Iron hammers like a spirit medium's."
When I reached Uganda they refused me space in a canoe.
So I went by hanging on the sternpeice.
They brought me to Uganda, then bent me over.
They found hair had filled my anus.
I climb a tree but have no fingers or claws.
They jog the fence chimes and I ring.
I am a throwing stick. I pass through cracks in shields.
I am a wooden crook and lie among tree branches.
I come from Bugabo Kingdom, Kabale village of Katwange.
I am a child of the Balwani clan of Mahugulu village.
I am of Byanjweli river of Mashaija.
I am of Mikimba and Ntule.
And Kantema Kashamba and Kantundu of Mabiga.
I am the Gatherer.
I trust in the snake, the Agitated one, the White spot.
Conquor, Provider! These spears are yours.
[The singer resumes in a different melody and poetic form]
Push on, children.
Push on, children.
Push on, Mukotani,
Thunder that splits rocks.
Push on, Bwhama,
Bull that does not worry about his appearance.
And him I call child.
Push on, Lwaijumba Kikuba,
He who never swallows a grudge,
He came from Kalisa.
Push on, Munyaninga,
Giant spear of Nyababa.
Push on, child of Kayagi Kimuli,
And that one a child, a child.
Push on, Muntu of Kashaija,
Wheelings-about of a bull.
Kalokola Lusanju,
And that one is a child.
They call him Kalokola,
Ihuwe tree of Busholo,
Mujuju tree . . .
And Lwendulu Omuhinda,
And that one is a child.
Zili Omukwoga,
And that one is a child.
They are crying, they are crying.
Push on, push on, children.
